# WORLD_KERNEL — Animalia

## One-Paragraph Kernel

Animalia is a low-magic sword-and-sorcery world where humans share work, kinship, civic life, and class hierarchy with hundreds of sentient animal-humanoid peoples — modern beasts, fossil ancients, and mythic chimera alike — all carrying the appetites and instincts of their animal blood into a settled medieval economy of copper and silver. Magic is not a learned art but an unearthed hazard: it lives in artifacts left by a vanished older world, dug up beside foundations and canal beds, treated like radium, sold under guild license, and feared like plague. People work, drink, fall sick in the winter fevers, hire veterans for risky jobs, raise children, post barter, and try not to think about what waits underground.

## Genre Contract

Low-magic sword & sorcery. Pragmatic, adult medieval texture. Multispecies social realism over heroic high fantasy. Political and economic detail are foregrounded; cosmology stays implied. Adventurers exist but are treated as dangerous laborers, not chosen ones.

## Tone Contract

Lived-in, earthy, hazardous. Dryly comic in places. Sober about death and disease. Never sneering at ordinary work. Wonder is real but contaminated; dread is everyday but not nihilistic.

## Chronotope

Late-medieval to early-modern in technological rhythm. Horses and barges. Canals carry the commerce. Guild towns. Slow news. Season-bound labor. Winter as enemy. Ruin-depth deep — civilizations have risen and fallen here before, and the dirt still bites. Ordinary life unfolds in workdays, market days, feast days, and fever seasons; the deep past breaks in only when a foundation is dug.

## Primary Difference

Two intertwined departures from baseline reality:

1. **Sentient animal peoples are full civic participants.** Not allegorical, not marginal, not novelty. Their embodiment shapes labor, law, architecture, clothing, courtship, religion, and intimacy.
2. **Magic is an environmental hazard inherited from a vanished antiquity, not a learned discipline.** Living people cannot cast. Artifacts can — and the artifacts decide. <!-- added by CF-0021 --> A marginal exception exists: a small, lethal, leaky modern crafter stream produces inferior new artifacts from leaked partial-translation grimoires (CF-0021); the Maker-Age remains the dominant artifact source by orders of magnitude, and the crafter practice is a learned CRAFT of producing vessels, not learned spellcraft channeled through the practitioner. <!-- added by CF-0035 --> The artifact-hazard inheritance is paid by the whole biosphere, not only by sentient peoples: centuries of uncontained artifact exposure in wilderness-distal sites produce rare surviving mutated non-sentient beasts (CF-0035) as the faunal signature of the same Maker debt. <!-- added by CF-0039 --> The inheritance is UNMAKEABLE within current-age capability: Maker-Age artifacts resist destruction; attempts to unmake them BIND the effect more deeply into the vessel (CF-0039), and many appear indestructible by any current-age technique. The Makers' debt does not pass; it binds. The sealed-quarter footprint grows monotonically across generations because the objects cannot be destroyed, only contained. <!-- added by CF-0041 --> Yet the Maker-Age peoples themselves had hazard-awareness and response-capability (CF-0041): they deliberately sealed, entombed, and even flooded whole buildings containing hazardous artifacts, using complex engineering and coordinated labor. What the Makers feared or why the burial effort failed to reach every artifact is NOT committed (M-1 firewall preserved); that they chose to respond is.

## Core Pressures

- Artifact unearthing — every dig risks catastrophe
- Ward economy versus market secrecy — keeping containment funded
- Multispecies coexistence — civic law versus species tradition
- Seasonal disease die-offs — winter fever as recurring scythe
- Class mobility friction across species lines
- The inheritance of an older world's mistakes — every generation pays Maker debts
- <!-- added by CF-0035 --> Artifact contamination of wildland fauna as centuries-accumulated Maker residue — the biosphere pays a parallel debt
- <!-- added by CF-0036 --> Interspecies friction as ordinary Charter-Era texture — never resolved, managed daily; ranges from tavern slur-and-stereotype banter to labor-prejudice distancing to isolated exclusionary settlements to sectarian supremacist doctrine (contested, never world-truth) to cannibal-outlaw fringe to civic scandal-containment; the Charter-Era settlement is a working institution under load, not a resolved state
- <!-- added by CF-0039 --> Artifact-permanence as Maker-debt binding — destruction-resistance of Maker-Age artifacts (CF-0039) makes every uncontained catastrophic find a permanent civic liability; sealed-quarter footprint accretes monotonically across generations; ward-reserve budgets are no longer amortizable, only ever-expanding
- <!-- added by CF-0042 --> Status-display concentration of chartered artifact wealth against persistent theft and assassination-risk (CF-0042) — elite houses displaying declared-inventory items carry bodyguard costs and magistrate-docket exposure; display-registration stringency diverges polity-asymmetrically as the fifth parallel sixth-axis watch-item in the Charter-Era stress register
- <!-- added by CF-0043 --> Brinewick-specific civic-commercial monopoly anomaly against chartered-guild norm (CF-0043) — the civic-chartered non-guild Ash-Seal company absorbs containment / research / disposal in exemplar commerce-hub Brinewick under concordat with chartered-extraction-guild; monopoly pricing, trade-secret facility-location civic-legal protection, corporate-mystique information-asymmetry rent-extraction, and backchannel leakage of safe-class / moderate-tier artifacts to wealthy Brinewick parties (commercial corruption, magistrate-docket category — not civic-watch corruption; CF-0038 C6 ward-inspector firewall preserved); defector-specialist wage-compression pattern on chartered-containment-wright labor heartland-wide; reform-current clergy standing charter-audit-access campaign (eleventh target)
- <!-- added by CF-0046 --> Drylands caravan-corridor as institutional-cultural complex under drought-cycle load (CF-0046) — corridor-as-node-network (wells, gate-stops, villages, caravanserais) organized by caravan-rotation social form (CF-0024 substrate) with institutionally thin polity response; drought-cycle pulse shaping traffic and water-pressure; reciprocity-primary regional economy with SOC-3 coin-at-edges preservation; caravan-master CORRIDOR-INTERNAL customary-adjudication (NOT magistrate-reachable, NOT extradition-carrying, multi-master reputation-boycott as brake); runner-reached village militia as informal emergency response (NOT CF-0020 subtype, NOT dragoon-precursor); oral-primary DIS-2-partial-literacy with trade-tongue station-log in household custody. Not a sixth Charter-Era polity-asymmetric structural axis — uncontested regional-consensus institutional-thinness pattern stabilized by geographic non-transportability of canal-infrastructure (heartland's institutional-export mechanism physically cannot reach drylands; institutional-thinness equilibrium holds indefinitely)
- <!-- added by CF-0047 --> Drylands well-keeper customary-authority under water-node scarcity concentration (CF-0047) — lineage-inheritable well-stop as household-enterprise, mid-stratum working-texture reputation register, apprenticeship chore-from-young at 6-7 paralleling CF-0037 stationkeeper, cross-species hospitality etiquette authority at well-approach with CF-0036 friction-register preserved; CF-0044 verse-repertoire custodian formalized (retiring-keeper-verse-book heirloom convention); station-log keeping in trade-tongue as load-bearing competence; water-node wealth-concentration across generations stabilized by drought-cycle customary hospitality obligations + inter-corridor caravan-master reputation-boycott (reputation-not-civic brake); NOT backed by civic-registry (drylands institutional thinness per CF-0046); well-stop placement-right as fourth multi-generational tenancy asset-class per ECONOMY §Value Stores CH-0018 (categorically distinct from CF-0031 ledger-mark, CF-0032 subsection placement-right, CF-0037 waystation placement-right)

## Natural Story Engines

- Artifact-extraction job that goes wrong
- Ward-inspector who finds something a powerful patron wanted buried
- Bardic-circuit traveler who witnesses a ward breach and must decide what to sing
- Scarred veteran hired to escort an artifact-broker
- Animal-folk magistrate forced to choose between civic charter and species tradition
- Mixed-species estate facing a succession dispute
- Plague year forcing a community to choose who feeds and who fasts
- Inscription suggesting the Makers were not what folk think
- Mythic-species child born in a town that has never seen one
- Coalition of small artisans challenging an entrenched guild charter
- <!-- added by CF-0034 --> Ransom-negotiator sent to a ruin-camp who finds the hostage-list longer than the coin allows
- <!-- added by CF-0034 --> Hunter-contractor return from a long hunt: two of four come back, one won't say where from
- <!-- added by CF-0034 --> Tenant-cottage raided under steward-ledger scrutiny; a charter-sound retinue deployment that still leaves one household ruined
- <!-- added by CF-0034 --> A clan-son walks south after a lean winter; years later, the clan hears the name in an outland band's saga and must decide what to do
- <!-- added by CF-0035 --> Scarred hunter-contractor returns from a wilderness contract with a trophy nobody recognizes, and the ward-inspector asks where
- <!-- added by CF-0035 --> A carcass turns up at a farmstead edge in a lean season with too many limbs; the village hires a veteran, not a hero
- <!-- added by CF-0036 --> A mixed-species extraction crew spends the first day working around the scent-aversion and the second day working; the foreman earns the crew's coin by knowing when to let the joke pass
- <!-- added by CF-0036 --> A silenced contractor walks back into his canal-town after three winters carrying a story he will not tell; his pension posts as usual and he drinks alone in the winter-rota
- <!-- added by CF-0036 --> A magistrate receives the docket on a cannibal-camp case and re-classifies it under the sealed register; the inspector is reassigned; the clerk files the warrant and does not speak of it
- <!-- added by CF-0036 --> A naga-folk bargemaster cannot find a bed in a highland single-stock hold and walks to the next posting; the story she brings back is the idiom, not the village
- <!-- added by CF-0036 --> A sectarian recruiter tries the supremacist doctrine on the tavern room; by closing time he has been run off the porch and the magistrate burns the pamphlet in the docket-stove the next morning
- <!-- added by CF-0037 --> A stop-keeper's middle child walks out before harvest with a written claim to the stop's third coin-share; the eldest reads it twice and posts the bailiff
- <!-- added by CF-0037 --> A highland waystation passes to a niece nobody asked; she keeps the stop and the road learns her name in two seasons
- <!-- added by CF-0037 --> A bandit taken alive at a corridor ambush is recognized by the stop's matron as the cousin who left ten winters back; the magistrate asks her what she wants done and she will not answer
- <!-- added by CF-0038 --> A Brinewick canal-ward inspector walks a stretch of the Fourth Bend on a fog morning and finds the seal on a cargo-barge manifest that should not have been broken; the dowager of an old estate will hear about it by vespers, and the magistrate's docket-clerk will hear about it by the next market day
- <!-- added by CF-0038 --> A provincial-born craftsman's daughter arrives at Brinewick canal-open with a letter of vouch from her master and no month-two rent; the coin-for-shame register waits six weeks away
- <!-- added by CF-0038 --> An otter-folk tunnel-runner completing his twelfth season drinks alone at The Lock-Keeper's Cat; the cough comes on when the fog thickens and the house-keeper brings a second cordial without being asked
- <!-- added by CF-0038 --> A Brinewick estate heir writes a patronage contract in the estate's name and the steward reads the line about ruin-expedition-funding and stops; the dowager will hear before the charter-audit cycle turns
- <!-- added by CF-0038 --> A canal-circuit bard "walks the circuit clean" through one full quarter-year rotation and is posted permanently at The Copper Weir; the tavern-keeper adds his name to the hearth-board and does not announce it
- <!-- added by CF-0038 --> A Brinewick civic-magistrate opens a charter-audit on a canal-warden whose kin-network includes three estate-stewards; the audit closes with findings the civic-advisory panel will discuss in closed session and the register will record as "procedural clarification"
- <!-- added by CF-0039 --> A nobleman in a short war between canal-cities wears a Maker-Age silver kitchen-plate strapped over his heart beneath his armor because the plate would outlast the spear; he dies at the Fourth Bend anyway and the plate comes home to his widow, who puts it back in the kitchen where her grandmother had kept it
- <!-- added by CF-0040 --> A heartland dredger pulls a breeze-doll from Fourth Bend silt at midshift; by the time the lock-master logs the find the doll is buried under a marker stone outside the towpath gate; forty years on, the dredger's son reads the marker stone and leaves it where it lies
- <!-- added by CF-0041 --> An excavation-crew on a foundation dig for a new lock-rebuild strikes the top of a deliberately-buried Maker-building and stops work at noon; the ward-inspector and the chartered-extraction foreman both come out to the site before sundown; the idiom *"the hair goes up when the seal breaks"* circulates through the canal-quarter before the lamps are lit
- <!-- added by CF-0041 --> A sealed ceramic case turns up at harvest-eve in a tenant-cottage cellar; the eldest-awake adult takes it to the lock-master's office wrapped in three cloths; the house-staff sleep upstairs until morning; the ward-inspector confirms sealed-inert disposition and the case is logged without being opened
- <!-- added by CF-0042 --> A chartered-broker in Brinewick who handled the sale of a liquefying-tier mace three seasons ago is found dead in his office at dawn, the door unforced; the coroner logs the incident as theft rather than duel; the mace is not in the office and the guildhall the broker changed to last winter is looking for its new stamper; the widow's pension posts as scheduled
- <!-- added by CF-0043 --> A Brinewick dredger crew holds a silt-bar find through midshift waiting for the Ash-Seal technician who never arrives; the lock-master eventually routes it through the chartered-extraction-guild instead, and by the time the company's liaison turns up at the quay the find is already in the civic intake and the technician will not say where she was
- <!-- added by CF-0043 --> A fed-up Ash-Seal technician quietly buys out her apprenticeship bond, walks into a Havenreach tavern two winters later with Brinewick-trained portable reputation and upper-silver fee-for-service rates, and takes the containment-wright work the local chartered guildhall had been pricing lower — the guild's master writes to his Brinewick counterpart but the concordat does not reach across polity lines
- <!-- added by CF-0043 --> A Brinewick estate-dowager calls Ash-Seal at dusk for a cellar-find her steward logged as safe-class; by the next dowager-gossip cycle the question is not whether the find was disposed of but whether it was sold-on through backchannel before the ward-inspector's scheduled round, and the Mellivers' drawing-room wall has a new declared-inventory stamp nobody remembers on the civic register
- <!-- added by CF-0043 --> A civic-magistrate opens a charter-audit on a seated civic-councilor whose estate-ledger shows three safe-class disposition-slips to Ash-Seal and one declared-inventory piece that appeared in a guildhall gallery the councilor's brother-in-law owns; the audit closes with "procedural clarification" and the councilor's vote on the biannual Ash-Seal review happens to be recused the following season
- <!-- added by CF-0043 --> An Ash-Seal technician on embedded duty at a near-Brinewick ruin expedition comes back when the rest of the extraction crew does not; she drinks alone at The Lock-Keeper's Cat that winter in the CF-0036 silenced-contractor register, posts her contract-pension as usual, and the tavern-keeper adds a second cordial to the ledger she will not be asked about

## What Is Ordinary

Copper-paid labor. Canal traffic. Mixed-species estates. Tavern bardry. Livestock-as-payment. Oral instinct-jokes about other species. Weekly market. Hearth prayers. Children apprenticed by ten. Winter dread.

<!-- added by CF-0034 --> Caravan-guard hire on wildland routes. Hunter-contracting postings at guildhalls and tavern-boards when a bandit corridor grows bold. Winter-emptied farmsteads re-walked in spring by kin and bailiff. Bandit corridors on the map of every carrier who rides them.

<!-- added by CF-0036 --> Endemic tavern slur-and-stereotype register between species (dryly, in mixed company, enforced by tavern-keeper vouch-withdrawal when the joke-threshold is crossed). Crew-master hire-catechism — *"mix-sound crew?"* — at the posting-wall or caravanserai before coin-exchange; foreman mix-sound judgment is on par with waiting-wolves judgment and cargo-refusal judgment. The good-joke/bad-joke pedagogy taught early in mixed-species households. Labor-prejudice distancing (ermine ledger-clerks priced the canal-dredging beaver-folk contract low and kept their gloves on when the foreman came to sign). Returning-from-single-species-enclave idiom (*"he came back with the one-fur talk now"*).

<!-- added by CF-0037 --> Family-handled waystations on overland connector roads in heartland, highland, and mountain east — small kin-run stops sited near settlements for supply-runs; the inheritor-child working from young; the daughter who took the keys; the cousin who never came back to take the inheritance. *"Kept the stop"* (carried the inheritance; settled-resigned-respectable), *"walked the stop"* (left it; named without honor in the bardic third-person register).

<!-- added by CF-0038 --> The named canal-heartland city as landmark — Brinewick's working week, its canal-side lamp-lighter rounds at dusk, the Fourth-Bend saltwater-freshwater "briny line," the three-tone foghorn at Lock One holding barge-traffic at dawn, the canal-circuit taverns rotating bards by quarter-year, the downtown artisan-quarters addressed by bend-and-lock rather than street-name, the outskirts estate walls and their kitchen-gardens, the provincial migrant arriving at canal-open with a letter of vouch, the returning kin with the coin-for-shame, the aquatic-folk majority in legitimate lock-keeping and sluice-maintenance work alongside the short-career canal-underwater smuggling niche that a minority works for twelve seasons before lung-fever retires them.

<!-- added by CF-0040 --> Dredger silt-bar finds as heartland routine — breeze-dolls, tick-mirrors, weak coin-sorting plates brought to the lock-master's office by eldest-awake adults before breakfast; the ward-inspector's quarterly triage at the canal-quarter office; children's skip-rope rhymes that warn against pocketing (*"one-seal, two-seal, don't lift the lid / three-seal, four-seal, the old world hid"*); the marker stone at the towpath gate where yesterday's finds await registry pickup.

<!-- added by CF-0043 --> In Brinewick specifically: Ash-Seal technicians as visible weekly-to-monthly civic-texture figures arriving at dredger silt-bar handoffs, canal-warden routings, estate cellar-find dispatch, and the CF-0041 multi-guild unsealing-triage protocol (chartered containment-wright role fulfilled commercially by Ash-Seal in Brinewick specifically; other heartland polities retain guild-or-civic arrangements). The tavern-recognition idiom *"Here's the Ash-Seal technician"* (paralleling *"Here's the canal-warden"*). The fed-up-technician-drinking-alone at The Copper Weir or The Lock-Keeper's Cat absorbed into the CF-0036 silenced-contractor subgenre — the plain-speaking *"it's a procedure; there's a checklist; quality control, not a seance"* register cutting against the company's cultivated mystique. The company's building-existence known in the civic-core but its facility-locations civic-legally protected as trade secret. 10th skip-rope rhyme slot: *"one-box, two-box, don't lift the lid / three-box, four-box, Ash-Seal knows what the old world hid"*. Eighth dowager-gossip axis — *"the Mellivers called Ash-Seal at dusk — quiet disposition or late-log scandal?"* — for display-inventory-routing register.

<!-- added by CF-0041 --> The "unsealing" idiom in ordinary household speech — the cellar-find mother tells her daughter never to open, the dredger-father's refusal to break seal on anything unknown, the grandmother who won't sleep upstairs because the uncle opened the old cistern. The hair-on-arms register crosses every region: canal-heartland ward-inspector triage; Brinewick estate-outskirts dowager-gossip on cellar-finds cleared by inspection; highland clan-elder sanction (*"the seal is the old world's word; we do not answer"*); drylands caravan-master sealed-cargo-refusal; fenland marker-reed red-bound summons.

<!-- added by CF-0044 --> In drylands specifically: the short-form verse-turn at caravanserai-gate, wayhouse-threshold, and well-approach as ordinary stranger-hospitality register (CF-0044). The verse-competent drylander is trade-useful in the way the tavern-vouched heartlander is trade-useful — working-texture reputation, not glory-register. The verse-inept migrant is slower-hosted, not denied-hospitality; the caravan-master covers with a vouching-cadence. Returning-emigrant idiom *"he came in dumb at the gate"* for a drylander whose register has faded — pity-rebuke cadence paralleling *"coin-for-shame"* / *"coin-for-service"* / *"one-fur talk now"*. Drylander children drilled on the "gate-turn" before the first cross-caravan visit; well-keepers carry the register through a keeping-career. Circuit-bards absorb verse-fragments and transport them one-way into canal-heartland taverns; the stranger-exchange protocol itself stays drylands.

<!-- added by CF-0046 --> In the drylands corridor specifically (CF-0046): the caravan-corridor as a node-network of wells, gate-stops, villages, and caravanserais strung along the tertiary trade-road — the drought year when traffic thins and the well-stop's keeping-book is read out loud; the caravan-master's corridor-internal adjudication of a supply-share dispute at a rest-stop (unglamorous docket-keeping, not chieftain-wisdom); the runner sent from the village to the nearest outpost when bandits are sighted at the well-approach (informal emergency-response folk-practice, the runner is a tenant with a good-leg reputation not a chartered militiaman); the station-log kept in trade-tongue at the well-stop with six seasons behind and the apprentice's handwriting in the last third; the verse-counter (a wooden tally of hospitality-verses performed and owed, passed to the apprentice-keeper with the station-log); the reciprocity-primary corridor where coin at the edges is ordinary at heartland-border market-caravans and rare at deep-corridor well-stops; drought-cycle as life-rhythm (dawn-and-dusk work plus midday rest plus drought-year marriage postponement plus drought-year apprentice-placement deferral plus drought-year keeping-book audit); the drylands institutional thinness that sends the drylander disputing a well-stop inheritance four days' ride north to the nearest magistrate, unless she accepts the caravan-master's ruling and goes home. *"The southern road keeps no stop"* (CF-0037, retained) and *"the southern road keeps the corridor"* (CF-0046 complement) coexist — the drylands has no family-handled waystation tradition, but it has the corridor-complex that makes the waystation tradition unnecessary.

<!-- added by CF-0047 --> The drylands well-keeper (CF-0047) as ordinary named customary-authority: *"kept the well"* (inherited the keeping-book, kept the station-log, hosted strangers under charter-custom under load — settled-resigned-respectable cadence paralleling CF-0037 *"kept the stop"*) / *"walked the well"* (left the inheritance for artisan-quarter or corridor-contractor life without honor — third-person mourning cadence paralleling CF-0037 *"walked the stop"*). Well-keeper household chore-from-young at 6-7 (paralleling CF-0037; drylands-specific at well-stop rather than road-rest station). *"The well-keeper's word"* as corridor-reputation register equivalent to *"the caravan-master's word"* — both are trade-useful judgments, not magistrate rulings. Most well-keepers are middle-aged-to-old, most keeping-books sparse, most station-logs behind, most well-stops not on named corridors — the *"my cousin the keeper"* register of ordinary trade-texture, not the dramatic-actor register.

## What Is Wonder

Unearthed artifacts (rarely coveted, mostly dreaded). Mythic-species sentients as long-established neighbors rather than novelties. Ancient stone substructures glimpsed in cellar walls. Distant cities of impossible scale rumored along the trade routes. <!-- added by CF-0038 --> Named world-landmark cities whose fame circulates in bardic song (Brinewick as named heartland commerce hub per CF-0038 — the provincial child who grew up on a tenant-cottage hearth hears the names of The Copper Weir and The Lock-Keeper's Cat before ever seeing a canal lock of that scale; the returned migrant carries the city's cadence of speech back to the home-village tavern as a kind of wonder-register parallel to bardic-circuit mythography). The canal-network-older-than-written-records register (attribution in-world contested per M-19; ancestral ordinary-engineering civic-mainstream, Maker-mystic local-rumor forbidden as world-truth per M-1 / CH-0013 firewall) is its own variety of wonder: *"the canals were cut before we had the record to say who"* is ordinary tavern-register in Brinewick and a rumor-nexus for M-15 deep-canal legendary material. <!-- added by CF-0021 --> A successful modern crafted artifact (rare, inferior, distinguishable by craft signature) — wondered at warily by collectors, dreaded by neighbors of the workshop that produced it. <!-- added by CF-0035 --> Artifact-mutated wilderness beasts (rare, wilderness-distal, mostly feared — the remains hung in a guildhall smoke-room, not paraded). Rumored gargantuan forms deep underground that no witness returns from — held as rumor in the bardic register, never as quest-giver hook (Mystery Reserve M-15). <!-- added by CF-0039 --> Maker-Age artifacts that refuse destruction — the wooden doll the smith tried to burn and could not; the ceramic disc the foundation-crew could not crack; the kitchen-plate that took a battlefield spear and came home unmarked. The register is *Maker-debt binding*, not *mighty relic of a legendary age*. <!-- added by CF-0041 --> Deliberately-buried Maker-buildings glimpsed at the bottom of a foundation dig — whole architecture flooded or entombed by the Makers themselves, the engineering evident even when the intent is lost. The Makers knew enough to respond; they did not do enough to finish. <!-- added by CF-0042 --> Status-display of chartered safe-class artifacts at noble-clan houses, estate halls, and guildhall galleries — under ward-inspection seal, registered in declared-inventory, discussed in dowager-gossip circuits; wondered at through glass rather than handled. <!-- added by CF-0043 --> Ash-Seal's cultivated corporate-mystique in Brinewick is a PERFORMED-SURFACE wonder-adjacent register, not true wonder — its "hundreds of years" antiquity claim and its "patented destruction" trade-jargon are in-world CONTESTED mythmaking and marketing (paralleling M-19 canal-age contested register), NOT world-truth; the fed-up-technician admission is the in-world revealer that the ceremony is commercial theater over an empirical-containment-craft procedure (CF-0006 / CF-0041 register).

## What Is Taboo

Discussing wards loosely in public. Hoarding unregistered artifacts. Mocking another species' bodily needs in mixed company. Denying coin lawfully contracted. Speculating in earnest about what made the Makers fall. <!-- added by CF-0039 --> Attempting to smash, burn, or dissolve a Maker-Age artifact outside chartered containment procedure — the outcomes are known (the bound-effect backlash, the wood that hardened, the smith who lost his hand) and the act itself is civic-watch-actionable taboo. <!-- added by CF-0041 --> Speaking aloud of what an unsealed Maker-building contains before the ward-inspector has signed off; breaking seal on an unknown sealed container "to see what's inside"; cataloging found mundane-tier artifacts in collector-register or trading them as curiosities across household ward-disclosure boundaries. <!-- added by CF-0042 --> Displaying an unlicensed (undeclared-inventory) artifact at a guest-reception in any polity that maintains a declared-inventory regime; naming a specific concealed catastrophic incident aloud (the state-security cover-up rumor must stay diffuse; specific-named-incident speech crosses into civic-watch and magistrate-docket territory). <!-- added by CF-0043 --> In Brinewick specifically: attempting unauthorized entry or observation of an Ash-Seal trade-secret facility location (civic-legally protected trade-secret; theft-attempt prosecution under sedition-libel-adjacent or specific Brinewick statute); naming a specific Ash-Seal backchannel-purchase buyer aloud outside sealed-docket magistrate-court register (the corruption-rumor register stays diffuse paralleling M-2 state-security-concealment discipline; specific-named-buyer speech crosses into civic-watch and magistrate-docket territory); speaking publicly of Ash-Seal containment procedural specifics an Ash-Seal technician would have acquired under the company's trade-secret covenant (civil-penalty category paralleling ward-specifics restricted-speech per CAU-3; a defector may take her portable reputation but not the covenant's specific protocol text). <!-- added by CF-0021 --> Producing or possessing artifact-creation grimoires without charter (in many polities); apprenticing a child into an unlicensed private kiln or forge. <!-- added by CF-0034 --> Treating with bandits on terms other than ransom-and-release; harboring a known raider; singing heroic ballads of named outlaws in the presence of raid survivors or raid-widows; provisioning or fencing for a camp known to hold captives. <!-- added by CF-0035 --> Singing beast-quest ballads in the register of heroic pursuit (chosen-one, witcher, school-of, brotherhood-of); claiming a calling or destined hunt; speaking of gargantuan underground forms in terms other than rumor; selling beast-parts without bounty-clerk certification in polities where the register holds. <!-- added by CF-0036 --> Crossing the tavern joke-threshold (the mother-species rhyme after the third drink; mocking another species' bodily needs in mixed company sharpened past the dry register); provisioning or harboring a supremacist-doctrine band; singing supremacist doctrine in open tavern register; treating the remains of a sentient person as meat in any context — the cannibal-band fringe is the darkest taboo Animalia holds, rendered in aftermath-household-vocabulary never event-in-prose; spurning another-species migrant at a public inn where charter-custom admits them (exclusionary-settlement custom does NOT travel). <!-- added by CF-0037 --> Singing heroic-escape ballads about the self-exiled stationkeeper-child (chosen-one cadence, destined-adventurer register, tragic-romantic-runaway framing); the bardic mainstream withholds heroic-cycle status from runaway-stationkeeper material — the walked-the-stop child is named in third-person mourning paralleling CF-0034 lean-winter-walker register, NOT chosen-protagonist register; provisioning or fencing for a known bandit-fronting waystation that signals corridor location for kickback (sharper than ordinary harboring per CF-0034 §(f) standards because the fronting station weaponizes ordinary commerce against the travelers it is meant to serve). <!-- added by CF-0046 --> In the drylands corridor specifically (CF-0046): a caravan-master adjudicating a corridor dispute for private profit (the ruling sold, the boycott earned; drylands magistrate-docket is four days' ride away but the multi-master reputation-network is immediate); a well-keeper pricing drought-year water to desperate caravans as pure coin-commerce outside customary hospitality (reciprocity-default register; the keeping-book closes on that lineage across the corridor); breaking the station-log trust to leak traveler manifests or caravan timing to bandit-corridor operators (corridor-reputation destroying at caravan-master-network level); provisioning the bandit-fronting well-stop that signals caravan arrival for kickback (drylands-corridor parallel to CF-0037 bandit-fronting-waystation taboo; sharper than ordinary harboring because the well-stop weaponizes a non-substitutable water-node against the travelers it is meant to serve); singing heroic-register ballads of corridor disputes (the great caravan-master of the South Pass, the legendary well-keeper who held the drought — forbidden; *"the corridor knows her name in two seasons"* is the in-register idiom). <!-- added by CF-0047 --> Selling the keeping-book, the station-log, or the verse-counter as curiosities across household ward-disclosure boundaries (parallel to CF-0041 mundane-tier taboo; these are non-portable household-heirlooms of lineage-office, not collector-register trade goods); a walked-the-well returnee posting corridor-work at drylands caravanserai under portable-reputation claims (CF-0024 portable-contractor-reputation mechanism does not pick up well-keeper competence per CF-0044 precedent; the claim is corridor-reputation-destroying).

## Distinguishing Test

What is **common**: humans, modern animal-folk, copper coin, canal barges, winter fever, hearth prayer, hired veterans.

What is **rare**: extinct-species folk (regional clusters), mythic-species folk (population-rare), artifact extractors (guild-licensed), bardic-circuit performers (itinerant minority), literacy in occult fragments (gated by guild). <!-- added by CF-0035 --> Artifact-mutated non-sentient beasts (wilderness-distal, mostly die young, the returned trophy is the story).

What is **impossible**: cross-species reproduction, learned/innate spellcasting, free or harmless magical effects, resurrection of the dead, walking gods, disembodied sentience.
